metadata.title: What Does Miedema's Boundary Electron Density Measure? Valence Electron Delocalization as the Physical Origin of n_ws
metadata.description: Surface tension of liquid metals varies by over an order of magnitude across the periodic table. Density functional theory (DFT) can compute these values numerically, and Miedema's semi-empirical model achieves +/-10-20% accuracy using the Wigner-Seitz boundary electron density n_ws, but neither provides an electronic-structure descriptor that explains why metals with similar bulk densities (e.g., Al and Zn, Delta r_s = 2.4%, Delta gamma = 46%) have vastly different surface tensions. Here we propose that the inverse participation ratio (IPR) of Kohn-Sham orbitals provides this missing link. DFT calculations on 11 metal dimers show that the IPR-based delocalization index delta_IPR correlates with boundary electron density (r = 0.84, p = 0.001), a result that is robust across three basis sets (def2-SVP/TZVP/QZVP: r = 0.72-0.84). Periodic slab calculations on five metals (Na, K, Al, Cu, Zn) with valence electron decomposition demonstrate that sp metals have near-uniform interstitial density while d-metals show strong depletion -- a 3.5x ratio between sp and d averages. Maximally localized Wannier function (MLWF) calculations independently confirm this hierarchy. These results establish that Miedema's n_ws is a proxy for valence electron delocalization, answering the question raised by Williams, Gelatt, and Moruzzi (PRL 44, 429, 1980) regarding the physical meaning of n_ws.
metadata.notes: Preprint. All simulation code, DFT input files, and data are available at the linked GitHub repository. AI-assisted research; full disclosure in manuscript.
